title: It Begins with You: The 9 Hard Truths About Love That Will Change Your Life
author: Jillian Turecki

chapters.title: Who's in charge? You are!
chapters.text:
Jillian Turecki will never forget that day in early June 2014 when the world as she knew it ended. Not only had her mother recently been diagnosed with terminal cancer, but her husband decided to leave on the same day she suffered a miscarriage. And to make matters worse, he told her he was leaving her over the phone.

It's the ultimate definition of a "bad day."
Yet somehow, she wasn't surprised. She had seen it coming after spending years trying to convince him to love her.

Does this sound familiar?

Far too many people approach relationships thinking they have to prove themselves worthy of the other person. But what you should be asking is whether this person is worthy of you.

Forget horoscopes and destiny; you hold the key to changing your love life by appreciating yourself first.

Sorry, but a happy, fulfilling relationship won't fall into your lap by chance. If you're holding out for a Bridgerton-style romance, it's time to open your eyes to reality. But the good news is that this love will find you if you understand the responsibility to change things falls on your shoulders.

If you're keen to change your love life and stop repeating the mistakes of the past, keep reading to uncover nine hard yet practical truths. And at the end, you'll find an actionable list to kick-start your self-love revolution.

chapters.title: It's time to look in the mirror
chapters.text:
Of all Jillian Turecki's truths about love, the first is perhaps the hardest to understand. Why? For so long, we've been told to wait for Prince Charming or a damsel to rescue when, in reality, it all begins with you.
Yes, you.

The bottom line is that your love life will never improve until you take a long, hard look in the mirror and understand your relationship with the most important person in your life — you. That's because you are important. You matter, and learning to appreciate yourself will make a real difference in how you see love, act in relationships, and repeat particular patterns.

You have to be the love you wish to amplify. ~ Jillian Turecki

It might be hard to hear, but every heartbreak or disappointment you've experienced in your past relationships also involved you. Of course, you didn't mean to hurt yourself, but you have far more control over your relationships than you realize. If you're carrying old wounds that create self-sabotaging patterns, they'll keep cycling until you do some inner work on yourself. On the plus side, it means you're capable of change and are far more likely to have a healthy and fulfilling relationship because of it.

Perhaps you've been cheated on before, and now you believe that all future partners will do the same. Not necessarily. But it might repeat itself if you ignore those screaming red flags and choose the no-good types. And no, all the good ones aren't taken already; you just keep picking the wrong people.

Unfortunately, not every relationship leads to that so-called happily ever after. But there's one relationship that will always have a happy ending if you keep your focus where it belongs — your relationship with yourself.

So, where should you start? With a bit of self-reflection. Ask yourself honestly how you might be adding to the things you're dissatisfied with in your love life. For instance, have you overlooked red flags in the past or avoided difficult conversations?

Awareness is power because once you know what you need to work on, you've already taken one giant step forward.

chapters.title: It's all in your head
chapters.text:
An active imagination can be good, but spending too much time inside your head is a recipe for disaster. That's why Turecki's second truth is that your mind is a battlefield.

The moment you fall into the trap of overthinking and assuming you know what your partner is thinking, your relationship is in trouble. When you assume, jump to conclusions, and ruminate, you're not communicating. You've suddenly assumed powers of mind control and an internal crystal ball, but they're wildly inaccurate. Unless you've somehow figured out how to hack into your partner's brain, you have no idea what they're actually thinking. Even scientists haven't managed that.

The single worst thing you can utter is the classic, "If they loved me, they would …" insert whatever action you think they would do. The moment this enters the picture, you've got problems. Why not try communicating instead? Yes, that word again. Take responsibility for the situation by having a calm conversation and asking them about it; tell them you need more help or emotional support. It can be hard to show your vulnerability, but without it, you're stuck.

Pay attention to your thoughts and notice when you're ruminating. Take a breath and come up with a positive alternative.

Moving on, can you guess what the third truth is? It's that lust is very different from love. When you first meet someone, the feelings can be intense, and you might start believing those fairytales about love at first sight. Unfortunately, that's not how it works. That energy and spark? That's all lust.

The problem is that you think you've stumbled upon the one person who will make life unforgettable again, forgetting they're just showing their best self. You find yourself desperate to see them again, almost like you're addicted to that surge of energy when you meet. Then suddenly, it's like someone's poured a bucket of cold water over you. Perhaps they don't call as much, or they don't seem to try as hard. In this case, you've met someone ready for a connection but not keen on commitment.

It happens, and in the heat of lust, it hurts.

This moment is when it's vital to know your worth. Don't wait for someone who isn't as invested as you. At this point, this person is essentially a stranger; why ruin your happiness for someone you barely know?

Instead, remember that the only person who can make you happy is you. There is no such thing as "The One," and that feeling of being alive can only come from within. And when you meet someone new once more, remember to stay grounded in your friendships, work, hobbies, and the things you enjoy. They'll either stick around or they won't, but you'll always be there for yourself.

chapters.title: Search for the hero inside yourself
chapters.text:
Have you ever heard the saying that to love someone, you first have to love yourself? Well, it's partly true. That's why Turecki's fourth truth is that you must love yourself, or at least work toward it. The aim is for your self-love to be stronger than your need for someone else's love.

So, what does it mean to love yourself? It means accepting who you are. The problem is that you're with yourself 24/7, so you know every mistake you've made, every thought you've had, and yes, even that dream from last night. You know you're not perfect, but here's the thing: nobody else is either. So, instead of looking in the mirror wishing you were thinner, curvier, taller, smaller, or whatever other adjective you're thinking, stop.

Ask yourself whether you would speak to your best friend how you speak to yourself.

The tricky part is that once we start this habit of nitpicking ourselves, we do it to our potential partners, too. We start blaming them for not doing what we think they should do rather than appreciating them for who they are.

It's hard to love yourself if you've spent years tearing yourself down, but give it time. Allow yourself to be completely, 100% who you are. Stop trying to please others and do what makes you feel comfortable. Eventually, you'll realize it feels so much better than trying to tick everyone else's validation boxes.

This point leads us smoothly to Turecki's fifth truth: to speak out and tell the truth.

You'll find it much easier to speak out and say what you need when you're unapologetically yourself. Turecki recalls a date with her ex-husband when he seemed distant, causing her to feel anxious and worried. She spent the whole night wondering what she'd done wrong. But she now realizes that she should have told him that it's not okay to ignore her, and he should tell her what is bothering him in the future. Instead, she kept quiet, something they both did for the remainder of their relationship.

Always remember that your needs and boundaries are just as important as anyone else's. It takes courage to say what you need, but it's the healthiest option. So, say it. If you feel anxious, use your breath to ground yourself and focus on blame-free language, such as "I didn't like how tonight made me feel. I'd like to talk about it."

People avoid the uncomfortable conversations to "keep the peace." But peace isn't the goal of a relationship. Love is. ~ Jillian Turecki

While all of this can seem overwhelming, don't worry. At the end of this summary, you'll find practical steps to start focusing on and loving yourself. When you achieve that, your relationships will become infinitely easier.

chapters.title: Take me as I am or not at all
chapters.text:
Have you ever met someone who seemed too good to be true? So perfect that it felt like they were purposely designed for your life? While we'd all love to believe this happens, it occurs because we try to be our best selves whenever we meet someone new. You've probably done it before, and others have certainly done it to you.

But it never lasts, does it? Once we get comfortable, we stop trying so hard; we relax a little, and that's when the problems start. That's why the sixth truth is that you must always be your best self. And no, that doesn't mean being fake or trying too hard; it means showing up authentically and never pretending just to make someone like you. Just be you.

Turecki explains that we all have a higher and lower self, and we should choose our higher self every single time. The higher self is the sensible part; it has discipline and knows the right path. Our lower self is often childlike, easily hurt, and craves approval. This vulnerable part is also easily sabotaged, so it's essential to understand what your inner triggers are.

What do you do whenever you feel hurt? Do you retreat? Soothe yourself with food or excessive spending? Do you pull away? Once you understand your behaviors, you can notice when they're happening and allow your higher self to take over.

You can build this self-awareness daily by taking a few moments for yourself and sitting in silence. Also, remember to move your body regularly and don't overdo it with coffee and sugary foods. Sorry, but they don't help.

All of this matters because truth number seven is that you can't convince another person to love you.

A lot of the blame falls at Hollywood's feet. We see it time and time again in movies: two people meet, and one's ready for a commitment while the other just wants some fun. One falls in love and tries to convince the other that they're lovable. In the end, it works.

Don't fall for it. That's not real life. If someone tells you they don't want a relationship right now, believe them. If their actions show they're not interested, let them go.

Love isn't a riddle to decipher, and it's not a game you must try to win.

If you describe your relationship as "complicated," it's time to walk away. You're in a situationship that will only bring pain and heartache. Recognize that your limiting beliefs keep you stuck in places that make you miserable. Choose happiness.

Did you know? Studies have shown that women from the UK, US, and Europe have an average of seven sexual partners in their lifetimes, while men have an average of eight.

chapters.title: Be your own safety net
chapters.text:
Sometimes, life feels like a scene from The Matrix, and it's hard to know which way is up or down. In those times, an anchor can help you stay grounded and safe, which is necessary since the eighth truth is that no one else is coming to save you. And you shouldn't want them to either.

We all dream about that person who'll come along to make everything better, put a smile on our faces, and complete us somehow. But don't wait around. The only person who can do all of that is you. After all, everyone on this planet is flawed, so why wait for another imperfect person to fix things?

A relationship is supposed to enhance your life and existing happiness, not create it from scratch.

This situation often arises because most people are either fixers or rescuers. These terms mean you're either waiting to be saved or trying to fix someone who seems broken. Neither option leads to a healthy union. Ultimately, your problems will still be there, whether you're trying to sort out someone else's issues or hoping to be rescued from your own.

When you meet someone, keep your expectations as grounded and realistic as possible. Rather than having your head in the clouds, focus on your purpose, whether it's something like your job, hobby, or family.

Finally, the ninth truth is that you must make peace with your parents. Even if you think you don't have issues with your mother or father, consider how your upbringing shapes your approach to relationships today.

Turecki realized that her troubled relationship with her father heavily influenced the mistakes she made in her past relationships. He was a complex man dealing with bipolar disorder, dark moods, and narcissism, and was often abusive toward her mother. Looking back, she realized she'd unconsciously chosen moody men with passive-aggressive tendencies, just like her father. One partner was even abusive toward her.

It wasn't until she realized her father's influence on her love life that she could move beyond it. After all, we tend to walk toward the things we're familiar with, whether positive or negative.

Unfortunately, life doesn't always give us what we deserve, including the love and care we should get from our parents. But that doesn't have to be the end of the story. If you can, try to reframe the situation and understand why your parents acted as they did. This method allows you to see things from a new perspective. You don't have to forgive them, but you can let go of the past and start fresh.

chapters.title: Try this
chapters.text:
The path to a better you starts with small, intentional changes. Here are some practical steps you can apply starting today:

• Write a relationship autopsy: Take 20 minutes to list the recurring challenges in your past relationships. Look for patterns, especially those tied to your own behaviors or reactions. What could you have done differently?
• Define your core values: Write down your top five non-negotiables for how you want to live and love, such as honesty, emotional availability, or respect. Use these to guide your relationship decisions.
• Ask yourself the hard questions: Spend 10 minutes reflecting on whether you seek validation from partners or offer genuine love. Write down what you think makes the difference.
• Identify your attachment style: Take an online attachment style quiz (many available for free). Knowing whether you're anxious, avoidant, or secure will help you understand your patterns and improve communication.
• Create a self-love ritual: Dedicate 15 minutes daily to something that helps you reconnect with yourself, such as meditation, journaling, or a mindful walk. Focus on appreciating your unique qualities.
• List your emotional triggers: Write down three things that often trigger negative emotional reactions in relationships. Next to each one, note how you can pause and respond differently next time.

Your next chapter in love starts with you. These steps will help you align your actions with the life and love you deserve. Start small, be consistent, and watch your transformation unfold.

Read it till the end?
